title: About
text: Hi! I’m Ann Carlson. I am a sophomore sports media and broadcasting major. Here is a blog about the Husker basketball 2026 historic season.

title: Nebraska basketball had their most historic season
text:
Did Nebraska have their most historical season because of their offense?
Nebraska basketball has had the most historic season ever. They made it to the Sweet 16, won 24 games in a row and were ranked in the top 10. During the 2025 season they didn’t even make it to the NCAA Tournament.
Anyone watching Nebraska basketball this season could see there was a difference in Husker offense, specifically 3-pointers. Pryce Sandfort transferred to Nebraska putting up 129 3-pointers which was very helpful for the Huskers numbers. But it wasn’t just the threes that helped the Huskers during this historic season. Their overall shooting percentage and offensive rebounds also improved greatly.
Let’s look at some numbers.
We are going to look at data from the 2025 and 2026 season that was created by Sports References.
To see how Nebraska has scored more 3-pointers this year compared to last year we will look at the 3-point percentage. This will also be compared to other Big Ten schools by using a slope chart.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)

logs26 <- read_csv("https://mattwaite.github.io/sportsdatafiles/logs26.csv")
logs25 <- read_csv("https://mattwaite.github.io/sportsdatafiles/logs25.csv")


threes26 <- logs26 |>
group_by(team, conference, season) |>
  summarise(
    total3P = sum(team3p), 
    total3PA = sum(team3pa)
  ) |>
  mutate(season3ppct = total3P/total3PA)

threes25 <- logs25 |>
group_by(TeamFull, Conference, Season) |>
  summarise(
    total3P = sum(Team3P), 
    total3PA = sum(Team3PA)
  ) |>
  mutate(season3ppct = total3P/total3PA) |> 
  rename(
    team = TeamFull,
    conference = Conference,
    season = Season
  )

threes <- bind_rows(threes25, threes26)

threes$season <- factor(threes$season, levels=c("2024-2025", "2025-2026"))

bigthrees <- threes |> filter(conference == "Big Ten")

nu <- bigthrees |> filter(team == "Nebraska Cornhuskers")

iu <- bigthrees |> filter(team == "Iowa Hawkeyes")

mu <- bigthrees |> filter(team == "Michigan Wolverines")

ggplot() + 
  geom_line(data=bigthrees, aes(x=season, y=season3ppct, group=team), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=bigthrees, aes(x=season, y=season3ppct), color="grey") + 
  geom_line(data=nu, aes(x=season, y=season3ppct, group=team), color="#e41c38") +
  geom_point(data=nu, aes(x=season, y=season3ppct, group=team), color="#e41c38") +
  geom_line(data=iu, aes(x=season, y=season3ppct, group=team), color="#FFCD00") +
  geom_point(data=iu, aes(x=season, y=season3ppct, group=team), color="#FFCD00") +
  geom_line(data=mu, aes(x=season, y=season3ppct, group=team), color="#00274C") +
  geom_point(data=mu, aes(x=season, y=season3ppct, group=team), color="#00274C") +
  geom_text_repel(data=bigthrees |> filter(season == "2024-2025"), aes(x=season, y=season3ppct, group=team, label=team), direction="y", nudge_x = -1, hjust=1) +
  geom_text_repel(data=bigthrees |> filter(season == "2025-2026"), aes(x=season, y=season3ppct, group=team, label=team), direction="y", nudge_x = 1, hjust=1) +
  #geom_text(data=bigthrees |> filter(season == min(season)), aes(x=season, y=season3ppct, group=team, label=team)) +
  #scale_x_continuous(breaks=c(2025, 2026), limits=c(2025,2026))  +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    x="season", 
    y="season 3-point shooting", 
    title="The Huskers had a high jump in 3-point averages", 
    subtitle="They went from near the lower-middle section of being average to the upper half, just above Iowa.", 
    caption="Source: Sports References | By Ann Carlson"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )









Nebraska has moved from the lower-middle section to the upper half, putting them near some of the top teams in the Big Ten. This means that in the 2026 season they were making far more 3-pointers than in the 2025 season. The closest in competition would be the Iowa Hawkeyes who Nebraska lost to in the Sweet 16. Also included was the Michigan Wolverines who Nebraska lost to by three in the regular season. They won the entire NCAA Tournament.
But what about all teams in college basketball? Where does Nebraska rank out against other teams in different conferences as far as shooting percentage?


Code
seasonshooting <- logs25 |>
group_by(TeamFull, Conference, Season) |>
  summarise(
    seasonteamfg = sum(TeamFG, na.rm=TRUE), 
    seasonteamfga = sum(TeamFGA, na.rm=TRUE),
    seasonopponentfg= sum(OpponentFG, na.rm=TRUE),
    seasonopponentfga= sum(OpponentFGA, na.rm=TRUE),
  ) |>
  mutate(
    seasonteamfgpct = seasonteamfg/seasonteamfga,
    seasonopponentfgpct = seasonopponentfg/seasonopponentfga, 
      ) |> ungroup()


nu <- seasonshooting |> filter(TeamFull=="Nebraska Cornhuskers")
mu <- seasonshooting |> filter(TeamFull=="Michigan Wolverines")
iu <- seasonshooting |> filter(TeamFull=="Illinois Fighting Illini")
au <- seasonshooting |> filter(TeamFull=="Arizona Wildcats")
uconn <- seasonshooting |> filter(TeamFull=="UConn Huskies")

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=seasonshooting, aes(x=seasonteamfgpct, y=seasonopponentfgpct), 
    color="grey", 
    alpha=.3) +
   geom_point(data=nu, aes(x=seasonteamfgpct, y=seasonopponentfgpct), 
    color="red") +
  geom_point(data=mu, aes(x=seasonteamfgpct, y=seasonopponentfgpct), 
    color="blue") +
  geom_point(data=iu, aes(x=seasonteamfgpct, y=seasonopponentfgpct), 
    color="orange") +
  geom_point(data=au, aes(x=seasonteamfgpct, y=seasonopponentfgpct), 
    color="#0C234B") +
  geom_point(data=uconn, aes(x=seasonteamfgpct, y=seasonopponentfgpct), 
    color="green") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.4480238) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.4358616) + 
  labs(
    x="Team shooting", 
    y="Opponent shooting", 
    title="Nebraska is in the upper half of shooting percentage", 
    subtitle="The Husker’s finished in the same quarter as national champions Michigan and some Final Four teams.", 
    caption="Source: Sports References | By Ann Carlson"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )









Nebraska also saw an increase in offensive rebounds from the 2025 season, but they still didn’t look good compared to the rest of the Big Ten. How much better were they than last year? This dumbbell chart shows how Nebraska matches up against other Big Ten schools with offensive rebounds.


Code
big_ten_rebounds <- logs26 %>%
  filter(conference == "Big Ten") %>%
  group_by(team) %>%
  summarize(
    TeamOffensiveRebounds = sum(team_orb, na.rm = TRUE),
    TeamTotalRebounds = sum(team_trb, na.rm = TRUE)
      ) %>%
  mutate(TeamDefensiveRebounds = TeamTotalRebounds-TeamOffensiveRebounds)

nureb <- big_ten_rebounds %>%
  filter(team == "Nebraska Cornhuskers") 



ggplot() + 
  geom_segment(data=big_ten_rebounds, aes(x = TeamOffensiveRebounds, xend = TeamDefensiveRebounds, y = reorder(team, TeamTotalRebounds), yend = reorder(team, TeamTotalRebounds)), color = "grey") +
  geom_point(data=big_ten_rebounds, aes(x = TeamOffensiveRebounds, y = reorder(team, TeamTotalRebounds)), color = "red") +
  geom_point(data=big_ten_rebounds, aes(x = TeamDefensiveRebounds, y = reorder(team, TeamTotalRebounds)), color = "black") +
  
  geom_segment(data=nureb, aes(x = TeamOffensiveRebounds, xend = TeamDefensiveRebounds, y = reorder(team, TeamTotalRebounds), yend = reorder(team, TeamTotalRebounds)), color = "red", linewidth = 2) +
  geom_point(data=nureb, aes(x = TeamOffensiveRebounds, y = reorder(team, TeamTotalRebounds)), color = "red", size=2) +
  geom_point(data=nureb, aes(x = TeamDefensiveRebounds, y = reorder(team, TeamTotalRebounds)), color = "black", size=2) + 
  labs(
    x="Team Offensive Rebounds", 
    y="", 
    title="Nebraska offensive rebound is better than last year", 
    subtitle="Nebraska averages around 850 offensive rebounds where the best team in the Big Ten is near 1000. 
In 2025 they were at 250.", 
    caption="Source: Data Provider | By Ann Carlson"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )









Nebraska has a solid group of juniors and sophomores coming back next year. Assuming key players like Pryce Sandfort don’t enter the transfer portal they have a good chance of once again making history.
Was this historic finish because Nebraska upped their offensive game, especially in 3-pointers? The answer is yes and now the question is will they continue to dominate in the Big Ten next year and have another historic season.

title: Eleanor Dale carried Nebraska soccer in 2023
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggbeeswarm)

bigten_womens_soccer <- read_csv("bigten_womens_soccer (1).csv")

big23<-bigten_womens_soccer |> filter(Season==2023)

NU <-bigten_womens_soccer |>filter(Institution=="Nebraska")
MS <-bigten_womens_soccer |>filter(Institution=="Michigan St.")
NW <-bigten_womens_soccer |>filter(Institution=="Northwestern")
PS <-bigten_womens_soccer |>filter(Institution=="Penn St.")


Did one player for Nebraska soccer carry the 2023 team?
In 2023, the Husker soccer team scored 67 goals and made it to the Elite Eight. In their historic season, they also won the Big Ten Tournament and finished with a record of 17-4-3. In the 2024 season, the Huskers didn’t make it to the tournament.
2023 was Eleanor Dale’s senior season. She scored 28 goals to lead the NCAA with the most goals in a season.
Let’s look at some numbers.
We are going to look at data from the 2022, 2023 and 2024 season that was taken from the NCAA website.
To see how Nebraska’s goals compared to other teams in the Big Ten, we will look at the goals scored throughout three seasons. The chart is a slope chart to show the other Big Ten schools throughout three seasons.


Code
ggplot() + 
  geom_line(data=bigten_womens_soccer, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Institution), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=bigten_womens_soccer, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Institution), color="grey") +
  geom_line(data=NU, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Institution), color="red") +
  geom_point(data=NU, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Institution), color="red") +
  geom_text(data=NU |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season + .2, y=Goals, group=Institution, label=Institution)) + 
  geom_text(data=NU |> filter(Season == min(Season)), aes(x=Season - .2, y=Goals, group=Institution, label=Institution)) + 
  geom_line(data=MS, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Institution), color="yellow") +
  geom_point(data=MS, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Institution), color="yellow") +
  geom_text(data=MS |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season + .2, y=Goals, group=Institution, label=Institution)) + 
  geom_text(data=MS |> filter(Season == min(Season)), aes(x=Season - .25, y=Goals, group=Institution, label=Institution)) + 
  geom_line(data=NW, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Institution), color="blue") +
  geom_point(data=NW, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Institution), color="blue") +
  geom_text(data=NW |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season + .3, y=Goals, group=Institution, label=Institution)) + 
  geom_text(data=NW |> filter(Season == min(Season)), aes(x=Season - .3, y=Goals, group=Institution, label=Institution)) + 
  geom_line(data=PS, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Institution), color="navy") +
  geom_point(data=PS, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Institution), color="navy") +
  geom_text(data=PS |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season + .2, y=Goals, group=Institution, label=Institution)) + 
  geom_text(data=PS |> filter(Season == min(Season)), aes(x=Season - .2, y=Goals+2, group=Institution, label=Institution)) + 
  scale_x_continuous(breaks=c(2022, 2023, 2024), limits=c(2021.5,2024.5))+
  labs(
 x="Season", 
 y="Goals", 
    title="Nebraska had the highest number of goals", 
    subtitle="The Huskers had the most goals out of any team in the Big Ten during the 2023 season.
In 2024 they were one of the lowest scoring teams.", 
    caption="Source: NCAA Women's Soccer | By Ann Carlson"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    ) 









In 2022, Nebraska finished in the middle of the Big Ten Conference with 31 goals. With Eleanor Dale’s 28 goals, the Huskers were the highest scoring team in the Big Ten for the 2023 season. After Eleanor Dale’s graduation, the Huskers were one of the lowest scoring teams during the 2024 season with only 24 goals.
But how did they compare to the top teams in the Big Ten between the 2023 and 2024 season as far as assists and goals?


Code
NU23 <-NU |>filter(Season=="2023")
NU24 <-NU |>filter(Season=="2024")
MS23 <-MS |>filter(Season=="2023")
MS24 <-MS |>filter(Season=="2024")
NW23 <-NW |>filter(Season=="2023")
NW24 <-NW |>filter(Season=="2024")
PS23 <-PS |>filter(Season=="2023")
PS24 <-PS |>filter(Season=="2024")

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=bigten_womens_soccer, aes(x=Assists, y=Goals),color="grey")+
   geom_point(data=NU23, aes(x=Assists, y=Goals),color="red")+
   geom_point(data=NU24, aes(x=Assists, y=Goals),color="red")+
  geom_point(data=MS23, aes(x=Assists, y=Goals),color="green")+
   geom_point(data=MS24, aes(x=Assists, y=Goals),color="green")+
  geom_point(data=NW23, aes(x=Assists, y=Goals),color="purple")+
   geom_point(data=NW24, aes(x=Assists, y=Goals),color="purple")+
    geom_point(data=PS23, aes(x=Assists, y=Goals),color="navy")+
   geom_point(data=PS24, aes(x=Assists, y=Goals),color="navy")+
geom_text_repel(
    data=NU23,
    aes(x=Assists, y=Goals, label=Institution)
  ) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=MS23,
    aes(x=Assists, y=Goals, label=Institution)
  ) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=PS23,
    aes(x=Assists, y=Goals, label=Institution)
  ) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=NW23,
    aes(x=Assists, y=Goals, label=Institution)
  ) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 29.45652) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept =31.52174) +
  geom_text(aes(x=50, y=35, label="Upper Half of Goals and Assists"), color="blue") + 
labs(
    x="Assists", 
    y="Goals", 
    title="Nebraska peaked in goals in 2023", 
    subtitle="The Huskers scored 67 goals during 2023 season. They only scored 24 in 2024.", 
    caption="Source: NCAA Women's Soccer | By Ann Carlson"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    ) 









This scatterplot demonstrates the difference in goals and assists between the top teams in the Big Ten from 2023 and 2024. Nebraska dropped to the lower third in assists and goals in the 2024 season. Northwestern also dropped to the lower end. Michigan State and Penn State saw similar results between both seasons.
Now we have seen what Eleanor Dale did for Nebraska but how did she compare to all the other players in the NCAA?


Code
players <- read_csv("ncaa_womens_soccer_players_2023.csv") 

playerswarm <- players |> filter(goals > 0) |> filter(conference %in% c("Big Ten", "SEC", "Big 12", "Big East", "ACC"))

top4 <- playerswarm |> top_n(4,wt=goals)

ed <- players |> filter(name == "Eleanor Dale")

ggplot() + 
  geom_beeswarm(
    data=playerswarm, 
    aes(x=goals, y=conference), color="grey") +
 geom_beeswarm(
    data=ed, 
    aes(x=goals, y=conference), color="red") +
  geom_text_repel(data=top4, aes(x=goals, y=conference, label=name))+
labs(
    x="Goals", 
    y="",
    title="Eleanor Dale led NCAA with most goals", 
    subtitle="Dale led the Huskers with 28 goals in the 2023 season.\nBehind her in The Big Ten was Sarah Weber, also from Nebraska.", 
    caption="Source: NCAA Women's Soccer | By Ann Carlson"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    ) 









Eleanor Dale led the entire NCAA with the most goals in the 2023 season. The closest player in the Big Ten was another Nebraska player, Sarah Weber.
Scoring 28 goals in a season is impressive and is why Nebraska had so much success in 2023.
This answers the question that one player can carry the entire Husker soccer team. Without Eleanor Dale on the team, the Huskers struggled to have an offense during the 2024 season and then did not make the NCAA tournament at all.
Leading the country in goals, Dale was named first-team All-American, first-team All-Big Ten and Big Ten Forward of the Year.
Eleanor Dale was able to lead her team to an Elite Eight and have one of the most historic seasons, proving one player can carry a team if they can score enough.
